attributes.stem: A 65-year-old man presents to his General Practitioner with a two-day history of dull, aching right testicular pain. The pain is worse on standing and he has noticed some mild swelling of the affected hemiscrotum. On examination, palpation of the testes reveals a collection of fluctuant strands surrounding the right testicle.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is presenting with a right-sided testicular varicocele. A varicocele is an abnormal dilation and swelling of the pampiniform venous plexus within the scrotum. The symptoms of dull pain, unilateral testicular swelling which is worsened by gravity, and palpable strands, the classic "bag of worms" finding is suspicious for a varicocele.

Varicoceles most commonly occur in the left testicle due to the anatomy of the left gonadal vein compared to the right. The left gonadal vein has a longer course to travel and drains into the renal vein at a perpendicular angle. Conversely, the right gonadal vein has a shorter course which drains directly into the inferior vena cava on an angle (bypassing the perpendicular drainage into the renal vein, which occurs on the left side).

A diagnosis of varicocele can be confirmed through amalgamation of clinical features, examination and, where necessary, doppler ultrasound. In most cases the diagnosis can be made on clinical grounds and any further investigation will not be helpful.

While most varicoceles are idiopathic, an underlying retroperitoneal mass in the abdomen which is obstructing testicular drainage should not be missed. Obstructing masses could be a renal neoplasm, lymphoma, or other mass impinging on the gonadal or renal vein. A new onset unilateral/right sided varicocele is suspicious for an obstructing mass and should trigger further investigation.

As this patient has only had his symptoms for two days, some form of investigation would be appropriate. The most appropriate plan of management in this patient is an ultrasound of the testes to confirm the diagnosis.

Ultrasound-guided FNA is not necessary to diagnose varicocele, as it can be confirmed with non-invasive ultrasound.

On finding the varicocele, most certainly the radiologist will at the same time scan the abdomen, looking at the kidneys for any renal mass. Obviously if a renal mass is identified, then further imaging, such as CT or MRI may be indicated.

An excisional biopsy is not indicated as typically varicoceles do not require any intervention, however, if it was required, surgical ligation or percutaneous venous embolisation is the preferred method of management.
learningPoints: A varicocele is an abnormal dilation of the pampiniform plexus in the testes, causing swelling and dull pain worsened with gravity.
A key clinical finding on examination for a varicocele is a "bag of worms" palpation
A unilateral/right-sided varicocele should raise suspicion for a retroperitoneal obstructing mass in the abdomen and should trigger further investigation.
references: Freeman S, Bertolotto M, Richenberg J, Belfield J, Dogra V. Ultrasound evaluation of varicoceles: guidelines and recommendations of the European Society of Urogenital Radiology Scrotal and Penile Imaging Working Group (ESUR-SPIWG) for detection, classification, and grading. Eur Radiol. 2020;30(1):11-25. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31332561/
Lung P, Sidhu P. Ultrasonography for Scrotal Masses, Benign and Malignant. Ultrasound Clinics. 2010;5(4):443-456.
